topic: Why does stress cause physical symptoms?
headline: Your Body on Stress: Here's What Happens
caption:
Ever wonder why your stomach hurts before a big presentation, or why you get headaches during stressful weeks?

Here's what's happening: When your brain perceives stress, it triggers your 'fight or flight' response—an ancient survival mechanism. Your body releases hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, preparing you to face danger.

This causes real physical changes: your heart races to pump more blood, your muscles tense up ready for action, your digestion slows down (because who needs to digest lunch when running from a tiger?), and your breathing quickens.

The problem? Your body can't tell the difference between a work deadline and actual physical danger. So it responds the same way.

When stress becomes chronic, these temporary responses don't switch off properly. That's when you experience persistent headaches, digestive issues, muscle tension, fatigue, and even a weakened immune system.

The good news? Understanding this connection is the first step. Simple practices like deep breathing, regular movement, and adequate sleep can help reset your stress response.

Your physical symptoms aren't 'all in your head'—they're your body's very real response to stress.
